reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: parental right to choose the type of education for one's child (Alaptörvény XVI. cikk), subsidiarity favoring existing maintainer autonomy (state/church/foundation), and the tradition of merit-based early talent development through the 6/8-year gimnazium track
reactions.interest: parents who have already organised their children's schooling around the existing selective structure, and non-state (church/foundation) maintainers whose selective offerings are protected by maintainer autonomy
reactions.public_good_frame: protecting a proven, differentiated path for developing high-ability pupils and respecting parental and maintainer decision rights against premature centralizing reform serves the public good of educational excellence and institutional pluralism
reactions.argument: An independent audit is acceptable as neutral fact-finding and preferable to reform pushed through without evidence, but only if its scope and governing body are not stacked to manufacture a pre-determined case against selection, and only if the statutory deadline is not used as a lever to force structural change regardless of what the evidence actually shows or of maintainer/parental consultation.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would oppose if the audit's terms of reference presuppose that selection itself is the problem to be corrected, or if the deadline mechanism is designed to bypass normal parliamentary deliberation and maintainer consultation once findings are in.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: merit as the legitimate criterion for access to advanced academic tracks, and subsidiarity/maintainer autonomy protecting church and foundation schools' admission decisions from uniform state mandates
reactions.interest: parents and talented pupils, including from modest backgrounds, who rely on merit-based assessment to access selective tracks; church and foundation maintainers whose admission autonomy is constitutionally and legally anchored
reactions.public_good_frame: keeping merit as the operative criterion for advanced tracks preserves incentives for effort and talent development regardless of background, which serves social mobility and educational quality better than substituting a group-level SES proxy for individual assessment
reactions.argument: SES-weighted quotas risk displacing genuinely able pupils in favor of a demographic proxy that the scenario itself admits is unvalidated and gameable, undermining the meritocratic function the gimnazium track exists to serve; compelling non-state maintainers into a uniform admission-equity rule, even at pilot scale, oversteps the subsidiarity and maintainer autonomy the current legal framework protects. Additive support (tutoring, preparation resources) for disadvantaged but talented applicants, layered on top of the existing merit threshold, would be far more acceptable than a quota that bypasses it.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support if the pilot replaced SES quotas with needs-based preparatory support that still requires meeting the same merit bar, and if participation by non-state maintainers remained fully voluntary rather than mandated.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: tradition and continuity of the 6/8-year gimnazium as an established talent-development pathway, parental choice, and subsidiarity/maintainer autonomy against extension of state place-count regulation to non-state schools
reactions.interest: parents and pupils who depend on the continued availability of selective-track places, and church/foundation maintainers who currently operate independently under maintainer agreements
reactions.public_good_frame: sustaining the capacity of a rigorous early academic track preserves a proven pipeline for cultivating high-ability pupils into future professional and civic contributors; shrinking that pipeline on the basis of a contested peer-effect assumption narrows opportunity rather than expanding it
reactions.argument: A legislated year-on-year cut to selective places directly narrows the options available to families who choose this path, resting on a peer-composition benefit the scenario's own evidence rates as weak and contested. More troubling, the scenario explicitly calls for new regulatory levers over non-state maintainers (subsidy conditionality, extended place caps) specifically to stop them absorbing displaced demand — this is a direct extension of state control over church and foundation schools that currently enjoy independent maintainer status, contrary to subsidiarity.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would reconsider only if place reductions were purely voluntary and demographically driven (e.g., tracking genuine cohort decline) rather than a legislated cap, and if no new compulsion mechanism were extended over non-state maintainers.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: preservation of a historically rooted institution for early talent cultivation, parental right to choose the form of education, and subsidiarity/maintainer autonomy over selective offerings
reactions.interest: parents who value early academic differentiation for gifted children, and maintainers (state, church, foundation) whose institutional identity is built around the existing selective structure
reactions.public_good_frame: the 6/8-year gimnazium system is a long-standing, historically rooted route for cultivating talent early; replacing it with an unproven comprehensive model risks levelling down opportunity for gifted pupils and eliminating school pluralism that serves families across the social and religious spectrum, which is itself a public good in a subsidiarity-respecting society
reactions.argument: The scenario's own evidence status concedes the Hungary-specific causal case for abolition is unproven and conditional on bundled reforms — teacher retraining and choice/catchment regulation — that may not materialize given the country's documented teacher shortage. Removing an established parental choice and a working avenue for early talent development on the strength of cross-national association, rather than demonstrated Hungarian need, is a maximal structural change whose promised equity gain the scenario itself says could evaporate if sorting simply migrates to school choice, catchment, or non-state tracks.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would reconsider only given robust Hungary-specific (not merely cross-national) causal evidence that abolition improves outcomes for all pupils including the currently-selected, together with binding guarantees preserving some form of parental choice and maintainer autonomy.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: merit-based tradition of the gimnazium track combined with subsidiarity — targeted support for general schools is acceptable when it leaves parental choice and existing maintainer structures untouched and lets schools adapt implementation locally
reactions.interest: parents and pupils who prefer the existing selective structure to remain intact, alongside general-school pupils who would benefit from added support, and maintainers who value institutional continuity
reactions.public_good_frame: raising general-school quality through curriculum standards, targeted funding, and reduced retention lifts outcomes for all pupils without dismantling the proven selective pathway that develops talent early — this respects both merit and subsidiarity by leaving school-type choice to families and maintainers while still investing in equity
reactions.argument: Of the scenarios, this is the only one that improves conditions for general-school pupils without touching parental choice or the traditional 6/8-year structure, which aligns with preferring additive support over structural levelling. Its acceptability depends on implementation respecting subsidiarity — letting schools and local maintainers adapt curriculum and clustering to their own context rather than imposing uniform central mandates — and on funding being durable enough that the gains are not illusory.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would move to opposition if the equity package were used as a stepping-stone narrative toward later abolition of the selective structure, or if curriculum standardisation and school clustering were imposed in a way that strips local and maintainer autonomy over pedagogical decisions.
